exemplar.title: To what extent are current levels of tourism in the Gion area of Kyoto unsustainable?

detailedExemplarData.title: To what extent are current levels of tourism in the Gion area of Kyoto unsustainable?

subject.title: Geography
subject.description:
**IB Geography** is a **Group 3** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **physical and human geography, environmental interactions, global challenges, and spatial patterns**, with a strong focus on **fieldwork, data analysis, case studies, and Geographic Information Systems (GIS)**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2025**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Geographic themes - structured questions | 2h 15min | 2h 15min | SL: 35% / HL: 35% |
| **Paper 2** | Geographic perspectives - data response and essays | 1h 15min | 1h 15min | SL: 40% / HL: 45% |
| **Paper 3** | Global interactions - extended essay responses | N/A | 1h | HL: 20% |
| **Internal Assessment** | Fieldwork investigation (2,500 words) | 20 hours | 20 hours | SL: 25% / HL: 20% |

strengths: Fieldwork question is clearly geographical with explicit links to the Butler life-cycle model, enabling testable hypotheses.
Precise geographic context: Gion district boundary defined with scale and feasibility for primary data collection.
Clear locational maps include north arrows, scale bars, study boundaries, and adhere to cartographic conventions.
weaknesses: Lacks exact grid references or coordinates that would further strengthen location specificity.
Broader context map could better highlight the Gion boundary for improved spatial clarity.
Terminology (e.g. âstagnation stageâ) is not explicitly defined within the write-up, which may affect reader understanding.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: All three strands meet the highest descriptors: a well-formulated geographical question linked to theory, a specific and feasible field site, and clear mapping conventions. Minor omissions (coordinates, explicit term definitions) do not detract from top-band performance, so the full 3 marks are awarded.
feedback.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

strengths: Investigation methods are described with clear relevance to the research question and Butler model indicators.
Data collection techniques are correctly implemented, yielding adequate primary data for analysis.
weaknesses: Explanations of theoretical rationale (e.g. choice of Pearsonâs r) are brief and lack full justification.
Single-day convenience sampling and lack of calibration details limit data reliability and representativeness.
Details such as sampling precision, observer criteria for land-use scoring, and app calibration are underdeveloped.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Methods are clearly described and linked to the research question, and techniques are correctly used. However, the lack of comprehensive theoretical justification, limited sampling scope, and omission of instrument error considerations place this work in the mid-band, meriting 2 out of 3 marks.

strengths: All primary datasets directly support the sustainability analysis, drawing on multiple relevant measures.
A wide range of effective presentation techniques (bar charts, pie charts, scatterplot, choropleth map) enhance data clarity and insight.
weaknesses: Data sufficiency is moderate: single-day collection and incomplete land-use table headings reduce robustness.
Figure conventions have occasional inconsistencies (misnumbering, missing units, citation errors).
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Data are relevant and mostly sufficient, and presentation methods are appropriate and effective. Occasional convention errors and moderate data completeness limit the score to 4 out of 6, placing it at the upper end of the 3â4 band.
feedback.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

strengths: Correct use of descriptive statistics and application of Pearsonâs r for inferential analysis.
Data interpretation links trends and anomalies back to the research question and Butler model, with plausible explanations.
Analysis identifies and explains anomalies (e.g. C2 intersection), demonstrating thoughtful engagement with unexpected findings.
weaknesses: Statistical reporting omits confidence intervals, p-values, and RÂ² values, limiting the depth of inferential analysis.
Analysis coverage is incomplete due to temporal and spatial gaps in data collection.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student applies appropriate statistical and descriptive techniques, links findings comprehensively to theory, and discusses anomalies effectively. While missing some statistical depth (confidence levels, significance testing) and full coverage, overall performance aligns with the top of the 5â6 band, justifying 6 out of 8 marks.

strengths: Conclusion succinctly summarises key findings and weighs evidence for and against the stagnation hypothesis.
Supports conclusions directly with analysis results, showing clear alignment with the research question.
weaknesses: Does not explicitly state whether the hypothesis is accepted, rejected, or partially supported, which would enhance clarity.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The conclusion is clear, coherent, and fully supported by the analysis, meeting all requirements for the highest band. A minor improvement in hypothesis status clarity does not detract from awarding the full 2 marks.

strengths: Evaluation outlines relevant methodological strengths and weaknesses with clear links to reliability and validity.
Improvement suggestions (e.g., larger sample size, multiple observers) are practical and focus on reducing bias.
weaknesses: Depth of evaluation is moderate; explanations of the impact of suggested improvements are brief.
Feasibility considerations (resources, time constraints) for proposed changes are not fully explored.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The evaluation identifies pertinent strengths, weaknesses, and relevant improvement suggestions, but lacks comprehensive depth and impact analysis. This aligns with band 2 performance, warranting 2 out of 3 marks.

strandSelections.reasoning: Two location maps (JapanâKyoto and KyotoâGion) include north arrows, scale bars and clear study boundary.  Figure references are used and the maps are uncluttered, adhering to conventions, so the highest band is justified.

The fieldwork question is clearly geographical, focused on tourism sustainability in a distinct neighbourhood, and it is explicitly linked to the Butler Life-Cycle model from syllabus Option E.  The wording allows hypotheses to be framed and tested (âto what extent â¦ unsustainable?â), satisfying the highest descriptor.

Bar charts, pie charts, a scatterplot with line of best fit and a choropleth-style map demonstrate a range of appropriate, effective techniques.  Layouts are clear and titled; therefore the upper end of the 3â4 band (score 4) is awarded.

Systematic spacing and use of phone app show correct technique, but convenience sampling for surveys and a single-day collection limit reliability.  Data volume is adequate but not âhigh-qualityâ across all sets, so the second band is most appropriate.

Descriptive statistics (averages) and one inferential test (Pearsonâs r) are correctly applied.  No confidence limits or significance levels are calculated, so analysis is solid but not âcomprehensiveâ; a score at the lower end of the 5â6 band is appropriate.

The student links land-use patterns, perception scores, and survey results to Butlerâs model, identifies anomalies (C2 intersection) and proposes logical explanations.  While thoughtful, some links remain tentative; hence a 5 within the 5â6 band fits.

The student pinpoints the study site to the Gion district, supplies a scale (100 m) study boundary, and explains why primary data can feasibly be collected there.  Contextual discussion of Kyotoâs accessibility and recent visitor figures shows good understanding of spatial setting, fully meeting the top-band requirement.

Strengths (large spatial coverage) and weaknesses (convenience sampling, subjective scoring) are explained with relevance to reliability and validity, but depth remains moderate rather than comprehensive, placing the work in band 2.

Multiple primary datasets are directly relevant to assessing sustainability; yet collection over only one day and partial land-use table completion mean sufficiency is moderate rather than complete.  This aligns with the 3â4 mark band; a mid-band score of 3 is merited.

The conclusion summarises major findings and explicitly weighs evidence for and against stagnation, drawing on specific results.  It is therefore âfully supportedâ, warranting the maximum 2 marks.

Most datasets are analysed, but pedestrian/noise data confined to Zone C and limited temporal coverage leave gaps.  Overall analysis is good yet not fully exhaustive, matching the top of the 3â4 band.

Methods (land-use, perception, pedestrian, noise, questionnaire) are described with purpose and linked to Butlerâs stagnation indicators; however, explanations remain brief and lack full theoretical justification (e.g., no explanation of why Pearsonâs r is appropriate).  This fits the âdescribed with clear relevanceâ level rather than the comprehensive top band.

Figures are numbered and sources stated, but some captions are inconsistent (e.g., Figure 10 referred to as â11â) and units are occasionally omitted.  These occasional errors place the work in the middle of the 3â4 band.

Suggestionsâe.g., larger survey sample, multiple observers to average qualitative scoresâare practical and linked to reducing bias, yet the expected impact is only briefly noted.  This matches the descriptors for band 2.

annotations.comment: Land-use table lacks clear headings and units; include descriptive column titles and define any abbreviations to ensure data sufficiency and reader comprehension.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: While survey factors cover relevant perceptual metrics, a graphical depiction of the â3 to 3 scale (e.g., slider graphic) would guide observers and reduce subjectivity.
Inter-observer variability is not addressed in the environmental perception method; include calibration exercises among groups to improve consistency and reliability.
Justify the selection of a 10-minute pedestrian count duration by referencing appropriate carrying capacity literature or standards.
Noise-level methodology should note app calibration and differences across devices to acknowledge potential measurement bias and improve data quality.
The fieldwork question is clearly geographical with explicit links to the Butler Model, enabling formation of testable hypotheses and aligning well with syllabus theory.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: Image caption notes authorship but lacks spatial conventions (e.g., north arrow, scale) that would enhance locational clarity; consider adding map-like elements.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: Geographical context introduces tourism impacts but omits precise locational details (e.g., coordinates, grid references), which would strengthen feasibility assessment for primary data.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: Map of JapanâKyoto is uncluttered and scaled but does not highlight the Gion boundary; adding a clear study-area outline would improve spatial representation.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: Contextual discussion of Gionâs tourism management measures is well integrated, demonstrating a good theoretical linkage to the Butler Modelâs stagnation indicators.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: The link to the IB syllabus Option E is clear, but defining âstagnation stageâ in situ would improve clarity and ensure reader understanding.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: Hypothesis is concise and testable, but specifying which stagnation indicators will be measured (e.g., carrying capacity thresholds) would tighten its operational definition.
Reference to âButler Model, 2024â in the rationale lacks author and publication details; ensure consistent in-text citation format and full bibliographic entry.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: Data collection over a single day restricts temporal validity; consider replicating surveys at different times/dates to capture variability and improve reliability.
Data collection locations map is clear but requires a legend distinguishing morning vs. afternoon sampling; add symbol definitions to improve usability.
Justify the precision and potential error of the noise-measuring app (e.g., calibration details) to support data quality and acknowledge methodological limitations.
Weighted land-use categories are described clearly, but specifying the criteria observers used to assign scores 1â3 would enhance replicability.
Acknowledges convenience sampling bias but could include a random component (e.g., systematic interval sampling) to strengthen representativeness.
Tourist survey description does not specify demographic breakdown beyond convenience sample; include age, origin, and stay-duration data to enhance analysis depth.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: Figure numbering jumps to âFigure 10â and text references are inconsistent; review and align figure numbers and in-text citations for clarity.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: Land-use analysis effectively links data to the Butler Model, but including quantitative percentages or ratios would substantiate qualitative descriptions.
Perception score map visualizes spatial patterns well; adding a legend with score intervals (e.g., 0â5, 6â10) would improve reader interpretation.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: Anomaly at C2 is well explained; strengthen by cross-referencing pedestrian counts or noise data at that location to corroborate perceptual results.
Justification for focusing statistical analysis on Zone C is valid, but clarify why other zones were excluded to support methodological transparency.
Pearsonâs r scatterplot is clear; include the equation of the line of best fit and RÂ² value to enhance the statistical presentation.
Interpretation of r = â0.49 notes non-significance but omits p-value or confidence interval; include these statistics to substantiate claims.
Linking statistical results back to the hypothesis is strong; discussing potential external noise sources (e.g., traffic, festivals) would deepen the evaluation.
Survey response interpretation contrasts other findings well; consider discussing how sample size and distribution affect confidence in reported percentages.
Conclusion summarizes mixed evidence effectively, but could explicitly state whether the hypothesis is partially supported or rejected to improve clarity.
Evaluation recommends more observers for qualitative data but lacks discussion of resource feasibility and time constraints; add realistic implementation considerations.
